description:
“The clues were all around,” says Swati Seraan @swatiseraan of By the Riverside @bytheriverside.in⁠ about this home in Bengaluru, “In the silk sarees the woman of the house wore to work each day, in their collection of antique furniture, and in the way they carried their heritage with quiet pride.” Brick was laid along the ceiling, silk was stitched into wardrobes, and the furniture that had been passed down remained where it had always been. While reviving the home, they took the cues from what they saw.⁠
⁠
Every material used in this house has precedent in South Indian domestic architecture. Bricks are rearticulated in ceiling borders and laid in alternating bonds — stack, herringbone and free form — creating rhythm throughout the space. Brass comes in through habit, it’s on coffee filter handles, window latches, prayer lamps, and cupboard locks—things still in use, still in place. You could be sitting in the carved-out reading nook by the window, surrounded by soft pink walls and a library’s worth of books. Or lounging in the bedroom, textured like a bowl of warm payasam—simple but quietly generous. Vintage sarees were deftly refashioned into wardrobe inlays, upholstery, and layers of soft furnishings. The fabrics came from the family’s own cupboard — sarees worn to work, to weddings, and to quiet mornings at home. They were folded, cut, and stitched into panels, cushions, and coverings. Their jewel tones like indigo, peacock green, gold and rust offer a material continuity that ties rooms together like a well-worn pallu. #EDHomes⁠
⁠
Tour the home the link in bio 🔗⁠
⁠
Text by Disha Kalyankar @dishakalyankar ⁠
Photography by Nayan Soni @nayansoni21⁠
